sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 25-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of knee pain and swelling after a fall from a tandem bike.
He reports taking ibuprofen for pain relief but experiencing persistent discomfort.
The patient denies any previous knee problems or issues before the accident.
Past medical history: The patient works in a children's hospital as a baby rocker, is active, and swims occasionally.
He denies any history of substance abuse, smoking, or significant medical conditions.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms related to the knee or other parts of the body.
Current medications: Ibuprofen (dosage not specified) for pain relief.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's knee is tender on the medial joint line, with a small, 1-week-old abrasion on the medial patella.
The knee is stable, with no deformities, and the patient can fully extend and flex the knee.
The valgus stress test is negative, and the medial and lateral collateral ligaments are intact.
The posterior drawer test is positive with 1+ laxity, and the anterior drawer test is negative.
The McMurray test is negative on both sides.
Test Results: X-rays of the knee show no obvious fractures, bone spurs, or other abnormalities.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) injury, possibly with concomitant meniscal or cartilage damage, given the mechanism of injury and physical exam findings.
Problem list: PCL injury; suspected meniscus or cartilage damage; knee pain; knee swelling.
Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen (dosage to be determined) for pain relief, and I will send a prescription for an alternative medication that can be taken twice a day.
The patient will use ice packs and will be fitted with a temporary knee brace.
I will refer the patient to physical therapy to regain strength and motion in the knee.
Follow-up appointment in 4-6 weeks to reassess the knee and progress.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up in 4-6 weeks to reassess the knee and progress.
